title: Arts Fest 2026 Saturday event, entertainment lineup
author: Saxtyn Lutz | The Daily Collegian
description:
With dozens of performances ranging from young violin and cello students to a Latin music sextet, there’s something for everyone at day four of the 60th annual Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts.

Here’s what to expect for Saturday at Arts Fest.

Art, as expected

Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition

The Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition will begin at 10 a.m. and end at 7 p.m. Artists from across the country display their original works, and shoppers can browse ceramics, glasswork, jewelry, paintings and more.

Guided tours

Two downtown public art walks, hosted by the Downtown State College Improvement District, will take place between 10 a.m. and 11:00 a.m. as well as 1 p.m. and 2 p.m.

Beginning at the Beaver Street Garage, the tour will take participants throughout downtown State College to learn about murals, sculptures and more. The tour also features recent works from Looking at the US: Celebrating America’s 250th.

Drawings and photos

Between 10 a.m. and 3 p.m., a Spin 360 Photo Booth will be available on South Allen Street.

Chip Mock Caricatures will also host a booth on South Allen Street. There are additional costs for both of these activities.

For the kids

From 10 a.m. to noon, The Music Academy will host an instrument-making station at the Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Plaza for kids of all ages to create their own musical instrument.

At 10:30 a.m., The Music Academy will hold an early childhood music class, geared toward those up to five years of age, at the Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Plaza.

title: Former Penn State men's basketball player Saša Ciani transfers to Sacramento State
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State player is joining his fourth team in his collegiate career.

Forward Saša Ciani has transferred to Sacramento State, the team announced Friday.

Ciani spent last season with the Nittany Lions, playing in 28 games. He began the year with four-straight starts but became a rotational player in the frontcourt after freshman Ivan Jurić took over the starting center duties.

The Nova Gorica, Slovenia, native ended his lone season with Penn State averaging 2.1 points and 1.9 rebounds per game. Ciani was also a team captain for the Nittany Lions.

Prior to his stint in Happy Valley, Ciani played for UIC during his sophomore year. He started his career as a freshman at Xavier.

Ciani was one of nine Nittany Lions who transferred out of the program after a 12-20 record last season. He's the fifth former Penn State player who has since signed with a mid-major team.

title: Off the record | Mind the gap
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Dear reader,

Every relationship, platonic, romantic or otherwise, is a direct reflection of the standards we hold for ourselves and how we are treated. Even though it’s apparent in friendships, it needs to be at the forefront of our minds when picking a partner. There’s absolutely no reason for you to settle for someone who doesn’t have a basic level of respect for you.

There’s a plethora of things that I could say inspired this, but a couple of months ago, I was walking in downtown State College, and I watched a girl fall off the curb in her high heels. The guy (I assume her date) stood there. He then looked down and kept walking. Yes, he just kept walking.

It’s actually quite repulsive, and I can only hope she’s no longer with him. But let this serve as a learning experience for us all. Your standards while dating need to be astronomically high. Not, “Oh, he held the door open for me one time,” but expecting the very best from the person you spend your time with.

Maybe this is hitting a little too close to home, and if it is, I need you to go ahead and do the thing we’re both thinking.

This doesn’t make you a snob, or picky. You’re allowed to want the best of the best. I know people who let their partners get away with the bare minimum, who let their partners get praised for things I would do for my friends with zero hesitation.

No, a gap isn’t taking a break from the person themselves, but rather noticing the gap between a quality that you possess and one they don’t. Most of my favorite people are in relationships where there’s a humor gap. She’s the funniest person I’ve ever met, and he’s a bore.

I wish this were a concept I, myself, magically created. Instead, I owe credit to the girls on TikTok who recognize there are no gaps in their relationships. They’ll post something with their partner that highlights the lack of a “humor gap,” “intelligence gap,” or “political gap.” This is the perfect phrasing, because if these characteristics are of value to you, the gap should be nonexistent.

Gaps and standards go hand in hand. If you aren’t settling within your relationship, then you won’t be encountering gaps. And if you find yourself with a gap in a part of your life that’s important to you, ditch the person widening it and find someone who fills it.

I’m not saying all the aforementioned gaps are going to be equally important to you. Maybe you don’t care if your partner is funny, or if they’re of your same political affiliation.

If you do care, finding someone who isn’t your complete opposite may be your saving grace. I think “opposites attract” is only somewhat true. There’s no way it’s going to work if you are completely different people, with different morals, interests and beliefs.

It should, however, be a deal breaker to come to a point in your relationship where you sacrifice your dignity. True character cannot be replaced by the facade of charisma, and if you have self-respect, the former should carry more weight.

And yes, I hear how this all sounds. All my advice ends with the same thing: Break up. I’m guilty of throwing around the phrase at any inconvenience, whether it’s a friend complaining over coffee about their situationship or a friend crying on my couch about their partner.

We’re all way too comfortable staying in relationships where compatibility is nonexistent. Forcing an unfulfilling relationship only burdens you in the long run.

These gaps are warning signs that it’s not going to work, and we need to be paying attention. The longer you continue to settle for someone who you know in your heart isn’t right for you, the further you dig your heels into a place you aren’t meant to be.

From experience, your self-worth needs to be stronger than your feelings for them. You need to be able to recognize that gaps of kindness, humor and drive outweigh the other things they’re showing you.

The gaps may even present themselves after the two of you are done, and that’s the greatest gift of all — showing you what you no longer have to deal with.

Here’s to hoping you reflect on the gaps in your life and act accordingly.

Until next time,

Miss Behave

title: Arts Fest 2026 Friday event, entertainment lineup
author: Justin Elliott | The Daily Collegian
description:
From the arts to education and improvisation, day three of the 60th annual Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts has a loaded program of events.

Here’s what Arts Fest has in store for Friday.

Kids Zone

A space dedicated to children’s activities will be open again from noon to 5 p.m. in the Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Plaza on 127 S. Fraser Street.

Both kids and adults can engage in arts and crafts, games and the painting of this year’s mural, themed “Where Hope Blooms.”

Festive Spirits Party

Big Spring Spirits will hold its yearly cocktail event from 5 to 7 p.m. at The Towers, 403 S. Allen St. They will serve locally sourced craft cocktails, beer and hors d’oeuvres, along with music from the local band Honey Pie. The fashion theme is casual, colorful and fun.

Tickets for the event are sold out.

Art, unsurprisingly

Hintzpiration art demonstration

A live art demonstration by Designer and Illustrator Brian Kappel will take place from 2 to 2:30 p.m. at the Hintz Family Alumni Center.

Kappel has worked with various brands, including Nike, Under Armour and ESPN, and he has grown up attending Arts Fest. The alumni center will also feature art from current students at Penn State and other vintage works.

Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition

Over 300 artists will be presenting different types of art from drawings to ceramics to basketry during the Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition, which runs throughout the festival until Sunday. Official jurors will award $20,000 in prize money to artists.

Friday’s exhibition will run from 10 a.m. to 7 p.m..

title: Fire departments respond to fire at Alpha Zeta fraternity house
author: Mia Debelevich | Managing Editor
description:
Editor’s Note: This story was updated Thursday evening.

Firefighters responded to a fire at the Alpha Zeta fraternity house on West Campus Thursday afternoon after smoke was reported coming from its roof, according to Centre Region Fire Director Shawn Kauffman.

Kauffman said contractors had been working on the house when the fire broke out, and no residents were in the property. There are no reported injuries.

The cause of the fire is still unknown, according to Kauffman, but is most likely related to the construction activity. It has since been put out.

He said there is heavy damage in the attic, and the rest of the building sustained heavy water and smoke damage. The property will be uninhabitable for "a period of time."

Pleasant Gap, Undine, Boalsburg and Port Matilda fire departments were on scene alongside State College’s Alpha Fire Company.

At 4:12 p.m., Penn State sent an alert urging people to avoid the area on North Burrowes Road between Curtin Street and Pollock Road.

title: Arts Fest 2026 Thursday event, entertainment lineup
author: Jackson Malenke | The Daily Collegian
description:
Arts Fest is here for day two with more activities, art and music.

Here’s what Thursday has to offer.

Art, art and more art

The 2026 Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition is back from 10 a.m. to 7 p.m. and includes a wide range of original artworks. Shoppers can browse pottery, jewelry, paintings, photography and more.

A new Live Mural Exhibition, “Dreaming American Futures,” is joining Arts Fest this year in honor of the United States’ 250th anniversary. The exhibition is taking place all five days. Designs are based on the theme of Dreaming American Futures and an excerpt from the poem “The Hill We Climb” by Amanda Gorman.

The Street Painting exhibition will take place on the east side of South Allen Street until July 11 between the hours of 10 a.m. and 7 p.m.

People of all ages can participate in the Little Art Alley where anyone can create a piece of art in a 14 x 14 inch square for a $5 donation. For a $10 donation, attendees can get a box of pastels to take home.

Craft Beverage Garden

The garden begins on Thursday, running from noon to 8 p.m. at Sidney Friedman Park. Visitors can enjoy regional craft beverages while listening to live entertainment.

Valid identification is required to purchase a reusable cup for $10 with the first fill free.

Kids Zone

The Kids Zone is located in Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Plaza and will be open from noon to 5 p.m. on Thursday. Kids can enjoy games, craft activities and help with mural painting. This year's mural theme is “Where Hope Blooms.”

title: Top athletes at each Penn State Commonwealth campus for 2026
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Sports are a key part of student life at Penn State, but University Park is just one of many campuses.

The achievements of those at the blue and white’s satellite campuses often fly under the radar, but the Penn State brand still makes noise beyond State College, stretching to every corner of the Commonwealth.

Here are the standouts from nine branch campuses, and runner-up honorable mentions.

Abington: Donato Incollingo, baseball

For a blue and white unit that finished an impressive 32-13 and went deep into the United East Tournament, Incollingo was the engine. The Huntingdon Valley, Pennsylvania, native slashed .399/.503/.842 with a team-high 17 home runs.

As for accolades, Incollingo was the 2026 United East Conference Player of the Year, an All-Region IV first team honoree by the America Baseball Coaches Association and D3baseball.com.

HM: Amirah Hackey, women’s basketball: 17.6 PPG, 5.8 RPG, 2 APG and 2.7 BPG – Lady Lions finished 20-7, United East Tournament runner-ups

Altoona: Riley Best, softball

After two seasons with Lock Haven, Best had a career year in her first season with a blue and white jersey, helping the Nittany Lions reach the Allegheny Mountain Collegiate Conference Championship Game, which they dropped to Penn State Behrend.

The Team MVP was named to the National Fastpitch Coaches Association Division III All-Region VII second-team, as well as the AMCC Player of the Year. Best led the conference in hits, home runs, RBI, slugging percentage and total bases, while finishing second in batting average.

HM: Lia Simanski, women’s volleyball: 267 kills, 22 service aces – Penn State finished 14-14, Simanski earned first team All-AMCC honors, was named Team MVP and campus Female Scholar-Athlete of the Year.

Beaver: Bailey Reig, women’s basketball

Penn State Beaver women’s basketball had an amazing season, finishing 23-6 and going deep into the postseason. The Lady Lions lost to Penn State York in the PSUAC Tournament and went on to reach the USCAA Division II National Championship.

In the blue and white’s Consolation Game victory in the bracket over Kent State-Tuscarawas, Reig scored a game-high 25 points. All season, the sophomore starred from the shooting guard position, averaging 15.2 points per game en route to earning PSUAC All-Conference honors as a member of the West First Team.

HM: Bryanna Neiberg, women’s volleyball: 240 kills, 59 SA – Penn State finished 9-17, Neiberg was named the only other USCAA All-American first team mention for Beaver outside of Reig and earned a PSUAC All-Conference second team nod.

Behrend: Dylan Barnes, baseball

The 2026 Team MVP put up all the statistics to back up the award this season, posting a .417 batting average along with a team-high seven long shots.

The Nittany Lions finished 31-12, and won the AMCC Championship Game in which Barnes had an RBI base knock. Penn State Behrend also appeared in the NCAA Division III Championship Regionals but failed to win a game. Barnes was named AMCC Player of the Year, an All-AMCC First Team selection and was named to the 2026 D3baseball.com All-Region 7 Team.

HM: Julia Jones (SB): Over 41 games played, posted team high.421 BA, also appeared on the mound, primarily as a reliever. Penn State finished 28-13 and was crowned AMCC Tournament Champions.

Berks: Talan Nguyen, men’s tennis

Penn State Behrend had a relatively successful season, going 7-6 and making it to the semifinals of the United East Championship Tournament before falling to Penn State Harrisburg.

The No. 1 singles player for the Nittany Lions all season was Nguyen, who was named to the UEC first-team as a singles player and the second-team with his doubles partner, Jazae Maina-Itegi.

HM: Maura Gorman, softball: - .299 BA, 10 RBI, .402 OBP (team best), 3.61 ERA, 54 1/3 IP, and 29 K. Penn State finished 10-21, lost to Wilson College in the UEC Tournament.

Brandywine: April Lewandowski, softball

As the lone UEC first team selection for the Brandywine softball team, she anchored a pitching staff that complemented a lineup that earned the campus's first-ever national personal awards.

Despite that batting stardom, Lewandowski was the most crucial to the blue and white’s successes. With a team-high 123 2/3 innings pitched, she was a workhorse in the circle, posting a 2.38 ERA with 116 strikeouts in the process. The highlight of Lewandowski’s season came with a no-hitter and a perfect game, both against Cedar Crest, once in the regular season and once in the postseason.

HM: Jayden Fogg, (MBB): Earned campus Male Athlete of the Year and second team All-United East honors, en route to Penn State finishing 17-10 and claiming a share of the regular season title. Averaged 11.5 PPG and 5.7 RPG.

DuBois: Natalie Bowser, women’s basketball & softball

Bowser was a two-sport athlete for DuBois, yet unlike some, she thrived at both this season. Primarily, her success came on the court, where she averaged a double-double, with 2.44 blocks per game, which earned her USCAA All-American First Team honors and PSUAC West Player of the Year.

On the diamond, Bowser posted a .354 batting average, a team high nine home runs and a tied team best 28 RBI.

HM: Isaac Tiracorda (BB): Penn State finished 32-8, with a USCAA Small College World Series appearance. The sophomore slashed .430/.551.729 with seven home runs and 38 RBI in 37 games. Named USCAA SCWS Postseason first team All-American.

Fayette: Kylie Stevenson, softball

Penn State may have only finished 8-18-1, yet despite the bumps, the Nittany Lions found their form in the postseason behind Stevenson.

The blue and white captured its first-ever PSUAC Tournament Championship, where Stevenson smashed three hits and two RBI. The Nittany Lions earned a berth in the USCAA Tournament, but couldn’t advance. Stevenson posted a .571 batting average with 27 RBI on the season.

HM: Jesse Lawson Jr., men’s basketball: Only 10 GP, 19.7 PPG, 3.7 RPG and 1.9APG, was named to PSUAC West Division First Team.

Greater Allegheny: Eric Valentin, baseball

In a successful season on the diamond, Valentin led the campaign from the batter’s box. The junior posted a team high nine home runs, 39 RBI and .420 batting average. Valentin racked in the personal accolades behind that strong year at the plate, earning USCAA First Team All-American honors and being named the PSUAC Baseball West Player of the Year.

HM: Linda Shepard, softball: Posted a .440 BA, 39 RBI, 9HR, 2.68 ERA, 86 1/3 IP and 51K. Named PSUAC Softball Player of the Year.

title: Potential 2026 Penn State defensive breakout candidates
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State will have many new faces on the defensive side of the ball, but it’s still littered with veteran players, leaving breakout candidates limited for the 2026 season.

Regardless, there are some budding young stars and other players primed to break out. Here’s a look at some of those candidates in Matt Campbell’s first year at the helm.

Daryus Dixson

Some could argue that Daryus Dixson already had his breakout at the end of his freshman year, but he has the chance to take an even bigger step in his sophomore year.

Dixson is expected to be a full-time starter at cornerback in a very experienced room, and with Audavion Collins and Zion Tracy around him, he has the chance to take that next step and be the lockdown cornerback the Nittany Lions have been looking for.

The “breakout” Dixson could have is more so him playing full time this year and showcasing more of the consistent cornerback that was seen in the final stretch of 2025.

Zion Tracy

Tracy is a consensus starter likely at the nickel position for Penn State. However, after his first full season as a starter, Tracy’s ceiling seems to be unlimited.

After missing time at the beginning of the year due to an injury, Tracy started nine games last season, notching 32 total tackles and two pass breakups. Tracy’s mix of speed and talent could put him in position to have a dominant 2026.

“I think Zion Tracy is one of the best football players that maybe I have coached, to be quite honest with you, in terms of talent, ability,” Campbell said in March.

Campbell’s view on Tracy showcases that he still has untapped potential that he could unlock during his senior year and become a difference maker for the defense.

Max Granville

Max Granville has already been with Penn State for two years, but he hasn’t seen any substantial playing time yet due to a season-ending injury last year. Regardless, the redshirt sophomore has always been spoken of highly.

This season, Granville could step into one of the two starting defensive end spots and be a key contributor in a role that many current NFL pass rushers have succeeded in.

Granville’s attention to detail and film study on former Nittany Lions could add him to the growing list of Penn State pass rushers in the NFL like Abdul Carter, Micah Parsons and Chop Robinson, all of which he’s studied to help his game grow.

Yvan Kemajou

The pass rusher on the opposite side of Granville is likely to be Yvan Kemajou. The sophomore showed flashes of being a strong pass rusher but never garnered enough playing time to fully showcase his potential.

Kemajou began to play more and make a larger impact during the end of the year. In the final two regular season games against Nebraska and Rutgers, Kemajou notched 1.5 sacks as Terry Smith let younger players get more burn.

Now in a position to be one of the main pass rushers, Kemajou could take a major jump and grow from his freshman season to add to the lineage of edge rushers that have come through Penn State.

Armstrong Nnodim

The defensive tackle room has a lot of experience, but Armstrong Nnodim could work himself into a starting role as a younger player.

Nnodim made his way to Happy Valley after two years at Oklahoma State where he made some impact in his redshirt freshman season. The defensive tackle totaled 11 tackles and two tackles for loss in 12 games as a rotational lineman.

The redshirt sophomore has already made a jump in practice against experienced offensive linemen, and his size will be a different look than how previous Penn State defensive linemen have been built.

“You could say he's an absolute bruiser,” offensive lineman Cooper Cousins said in April. “He's hard to move. He's 315 pounds, he's got like 18% body fat. He's a freak.”

title: Arts Fest 2026 Wednesday event, entertainment lineup
author: Taylor Mcgillvray | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts is back in State College this week for its 60th annual year.

Here’s what you can expect for Wednesday’s lineup.

Children and Youth Day events

Beginning at 10 a.m., budding artists can explore their creativity in Sidney Friedman Park and on the Old Main Lawn, where activities from face painting to jewelry making and interactive crafts will be offered for “Kid’s Day.”

The Children and Youth Day Sidewalk Sale, a spin on the annual Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition, will take place on South Allen Street from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. During the sidewalk sale, artists aged 8 to 18 from across the region will display and sell their artwork, giving visitors the chance to support local, young talent. All pieces will be original forms of personal expression, as encouraged by CPFA according to their website.

One of the day's biggest traditions returns with the annual Children and Youth Day Grand Procession from 3 to 3:30 p.m. The parade will travel from the Old Main Lawn to Sidney Friedman Park, bringing together colorful papier-mache puppets, handmade masks, local musicians and community members. Those looking to participate can pick up procession pieces beginning at 2:15 p.m., making it easy for children and families to join in on one of Arts Fest's most recognizable events.

At the Mayor Welch Plaza at the State College Borough Building, children will have the opportunity to decorate bucket hats and learn about sun safety with Mount Nittany Health.

Art, yet again

“Images 2026,” an annual exhibition highlighting artists from across the state, is an opportunity for attendees to view a diverse selection of two-dimensional contemporary art. The exhibition is free and open for viewing at the Schlow Library for all of July.

Remember to look up while walking along South Allen Street, as the annual Banner Exhibition and Competition will once again line the street with colorful banners designed by artists of all ages and experience levels.

title: Strength coach Reid Kagy talks player success at Lift for Life
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State players gathered inside Holuba Hall last week for the 23rd annual Lift for Life to raise money for Uplifting Athletes, a nonprofit.

After the players raised more than $40,000 for youth battling rare diseases, Reid Kagy, the director of strength and conditioning, spoke with reporters in State College about the event and summer workouts over the last several weeks.

Kagy also provided updates on a few key players recovering from injury. Here’s a look at what he had to say about these specific guys last Wednesday.

Tony Rojas’ recovery

Tony Rojas missed most of the 2025 season after tearing his ACL during practice ahead of Penn State’s trip to UCLA. His recovery stretched through the spring and summer, limiting his participation at spring practice.

As summer workouts have ramped up over the last few weeks, Rojas has gradually returned to the field.

“Tony’s progressing and (continuing) to do a great job,” Kagy said. “I’m proud of Tony.”

During the offseason, Rojas’ impact extended beyond the field. As the new coaching staff introduced changes to the program, he became a steady presence for returning players despite working through his own recovery.

“Everybody was experiencing change in the program, so I appreciate Tony for the leadership that he displayed,” Kagy said. “I appreciate Tony for doing all those things that it takes to create a winning football team.”

Even though he wasn’t able to participate in spring workouts, Kagy said Rojas continued to influence the program through his preparation and mentorship, and the linebacker remained actively involved in the team’s development during the spring.

“He was creating relationships, he’s helping other linebackers get through the defense, he’s learning in the meeting rooms,” Kagy said. “The same thing for him is we had an opportunity to take a step and grow as a team during the spring, and Tony was a large part of that. That’s what’s great about Tony.”

Armstrong Nnodim’s relationship building

Defensive tackle Armstrong Nnodim has made himself known among the Nittany Lions after transferring in from Oklahoma State both on and off the field.

“It’s usually somebody’s birthday every single day, Armstrong finds out,” Kagy said. “Whether it’s a team meeting or a team setting in any capacity, Armstrong (sings happy birthday).”

On the field and in the weight room, Armstrong has wowed the coaches with his work ethic and discipline, and he’s on track to play a large role on Penn State’s defensive line this year.

“He’s extremely talented, he’s extremely physically talented, he makes me look good,” Kagy said. “What’s been impressive is his work ethic.”

Max Granville adds depth

In addition to Nnodim, redshirt sophomore Max Granville is another player working toward a big role on the defensive line this season.

While Granville is recovering from a season-ending injury he picked up working out at home ahead of last season along with a setback later on, he’s impressed the coaches through as he’s worked his way back into practice.

“Max is somebody we were super excited about when we stepped in here,” Kagy said. “I’ve been super impressed by his work ethic. We’re really excited about what Max can do.”

Expectations were high for Granville before his injury after he reclassified and joined Penn State ahead of the 2024 season, during which he appeared in seven games and showed promise.

Now, Granville has his chance to breakout as a redshirt sophomore.

“The biggest thing about Max, Max is super humble and Max goes to work,” Kagy said. “(He’s) another guy that we've just been super impressed with from a work ethic standpoint and a guy that continues to impact this team.”

Rocco Becht’s development

After missing a majority of the spring season to rehab after shoulder surgery, Rocco Becht has taken a step forward in his return.

While he has yet to practice at full strength, Kagy and other coaches have been pushing Becht and helping him recover and improve.

“Every single day, we’re trying to make sure we build up some of that resiliency so he can play healthy,” Kagy said. “He’s getting stronger.”

Becht started three seasons for Iowa State, taking on the role of team leader. Although he arrived in Happy Valley only months ago, Kagy said Becht has already earned the respect of his teammates at Penn State.

“Rocco’s a leader and Rocco is a team guy,” Kagy said. “His toughness bleeds into the rest of his team. Even coming back from injury, he made an impact in any way he could.”

With his shoulder still recovering from surgery, Becht has been limited on the number of throwing reps he can take during practice. Instead, he’s worked on his lower body strength and scrambling ability.

“Roc is getting faster,” Kagy said. “Is he gonna run a route? No, but he’s gonna run, and he loves to talk about how he’s gonna run the ball.”

title: Film review | 2028 QB James Armstrong brings true dual-threat play style to Penn State
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State secured its third commit in the Class of 2028 when 4-star quarterback James Armstrong pledged on Thursday.

The in-state gunslinger ranks as the No. 10 quarterback nationally and No. 4 player out of Pennsylvania from 2028, per 247Sports composite. He’s the first commit in the class on the offensive side of the ball.

Armstrong had a solid sophomore season at Hopewell High School, racking up 2,232 passing yards for 21 touchdowns and three interceptions plus 799 rushing yards and 16 touchdowns on the ground.

Here’s a deeper look into his film.

A true dual threat

When the pocket collapses, Armstrong shows no hesitation in calling his own number. Whether a lane appears in the interior or outside, he’ll recognize the opening early and make a break for it.

Armstrong possesses blinding speed and the balance to stay on his feet even when outnumbered. He executes swift cuts and juke maneuvers, weaving between the defenders while shedding tackles.

In addition, he isn’t afraid to lower his shoulder and truck the secondary when there’s no way forward. That competitive edge is put on full display in the red zone, as Armstrong doesn’t shy away from diving forward for a gritty touchdown.

When given the time and space to make a throw, Armstrong showcases his talent for hitting receivers in stride up the middle and down the sidelines. His wind-up is quick, allowing him to get the pass off as soon as possible.

Armstrong’s solid when throwing on the run as well. His first instinct is to tuck the ball and take it himself, but he’ll look for a quick pass upfield when he knows that rushing the ball isn’t optimal. His accuracy remains intact regardless of whether he sets his feet before throwing.

Quick release, quick decisions

Armstrong’s game is all about efficiency. His throws can come in a fraction of a second, so he avoids getting sacked when his offensive line fails to keep rushers at bay.

Additionally, he appears comfortable slinging the ball from all different arm slots depending on the situation at-hand.

When being blitzed, his eyes move fast, and he’s able to locate the open target directly in front of him, making short to mid-range throws before the defense closes in. If Armstrong gets any more time, he’ll look for a deep ball downfield.

Over the shoulder dimes

While the short pass is his go-to when things get dicey, Armstrong’s over-the-shoulder lob shouldn’t be taken lightly.

Part of what makes Armstrong so effective is how he leads the receiver with his passes. It doesn’t have to be a slant route up the middle or corner route toward the back of the end zone, as Armstrong will meet the receiver in stride anywhere to keep their momentum going.

Armstrong does this best on straight routes along the sidelines. He executes over-the-shoulder passes well, hitting his receiver right in front of their inside shoulder while also limiting the chances of being intercepted.

Should the receiver get the separation he needs to make the catch without the defender interjecting, he’s either getting pushed out of bounds or taking it to the house thanks to Armstrong’s ball placement.

title: Borough Council passes ordinance barring borough collaboration with ICE
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
The State College Borough Council voted unanimously to pass an ordinance including provisions that prevent borough resources and personnel, including State College police officers, from involvement with Immigrations and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations on Monday.

Protestors gathered outside of the State College Municipal Building, where the meeting was being held, in opposition of the ordinance, codified as ordinance no. 2247.

Its passing prevents State College from enrolling in any partnerships under Section 287 of the Immigration and Nationality Act or “any successor program” that delegates federal immigration enforcement authority to local authorities.

The council also passed a resolution stating the borough’s principles on immigration enforcement regarding public safety and constitutional rights.

Other agreements for the sole purpose of civil immigration enforcement are barred. Borough personnel cannot collect someone’s immigration status — save for “determining eligibility for a specific service, benefit, or public employment as required by federal or state law or is reasonably necessary in the course of a criminal investigation for a violation of state or local law, or court order.”

John Hayes was the only council member to vote against the resolution.

Hayes said he saw the resolution’s purpose as being a "messaging bill” meant to advance a specific political position without seriously changing public policy. He said its text explicitly stated its contents were “aspirational and advisory only.”

However, Council President Evan Myers said the resolution was created because the borough cannot include specific things in the ordinance, like banning facemasks on ICE agents, as they’re not legally enforceable by the borough.

While the resolution can't be included as an additional provision to the ordinance, Myers said it’s important to have a document expressing the borough’s beliefs on ICE operations in local communities.

“There are certain things that we expect based on our home rule charter. Things that we expect as human beings, the way other people should act. And even though we can't codify them — because we don't have that ability to do that — we can at least state them again as a corollary to what we just passed,” Myers said.

Sylus Smith, a member of Centre County Democratic Socialists of America, said he understands the concerns of the resolution ending up as a symbolic gesture, but it would still have an impact on those living and working in the borough.

“It reassures members of the public, such as myself, such as my neighbors and particularly my many classmates at Penn State who are perhaps not so familiar with which ordinances have been passed when, that this council does believe in the human rights of immigrants,” Smith, a fourth-year studying political science, said.

Smith said the resolution and the ordinance were examples of how a local government worked to meet the demands of its constituents. He added that it shows the borough’s continued support for the communities it serves, making it “a little more than purely worthless symbolism.”

“Too often, we have politicians who seem to believe that their responsibilities begin and end with speeches and a vote,” Smith said. “Too often, they forget that as members of this community with such a high standing and with such connections to various organizations, that they can be part of bringing us all together and organizing us so that we can make more meaningful actions against injustices like this one.”

title: Penn State RB commit Aiden Gibson flips to Rutgers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Penn State commit has found a new home.

Four-star running back Aiden Gibson announced his flip to Rutgers on Tuesday.

Gibson, the No. 10 running back nationally, per 247Sports composite, committed to Penn State on May 12. Two days later, 3-star Chukwuma Odoh pledged, but now Odoh is the lone running back commit for Penn State in the Class of 2027.

The South Carolina native is also set to reclassify to the 2026 class, enrolling at Rutgers in August.

Following the announcement, Gibson thanked Penn State on X, saying, "Thank you Penn State for the amazing opportunity yall have given me and treated me."

The Nittany Lions are down to just 21 commits now in the 2027 class following flips from Gibson, wide receiver Jamir Dean and cornerbacks Semajay Robinson and Zachary Gleason Jr. over the past two months.

title: Former Penn State WR commit Khalil Taylor pledges to Nebraska
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State commit is headed to a Big Ten foe.

Wide receiver Khalil Taylor has committed back to Nebraska, he announced via the Rivals' YouTube channel on Monday.

Taylor ranks as the No. 84 player in the 2027 class, the No. 13 wide receiver and No. 4 player in Pennsylvania.

Taylor initially committed to Penn State on March 29, 2025 under James Franklin but decommitted on Oct. 10, a day before the Nittany Lions’ loss to UCLA.

The Gibsonia, Pennsylvania, native has consistently visited Happy Valley since the hiring of Matt Campbell, including a junior day visit on Jan. 24.

Taylor plays at WPIAL powerhouse Pine Richland, which made the WPIAL Championship in 2025.

The 5-foot-11 wide receiver returned to Penn State for an official visit on the weekend of June 5, but the Nittany Lions couldn't beat out Nebraska to land a top in-state prospect.

Tuesday, July 7

The Fretliners

This week, the theatre is showing SpongeBob’s latest adventure when he and his friends are sucked into an underworld quest thanks to the Flying Dutchman.

title: Penn State wrestling's Masanosuke Ono to wrestle at RAF12
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Masanosuke Ono will wrestle for the first time since joining Penn State last year.

Real American Freestyle announced Friday that Ono will face Ohio State's Ben Davino at RAF12, continuing the Ohio State-Penn State rivalry.

He'll join former Penn State star and current UFC middleweight Bo Nickal on the card in Cleveland. Nickal is set to face Max McEnelly in a light heavyweight bout.

Ono joined Penn State already holding an impressive international competition status. The Matsue City, Japan, native earned gold at both the 2024 Senior World Championships and U20 World Championships at 61 kilograms.

Prior to the start of last season, Cael Sanderson said Ono had one year of eligibility for the Nittany Lions. With the 133-pounder not wrestling last year, his matchup against Davino could be the start to eventually seeing him in a Penn State singlet for the 2026-27 dual season.

title: 2 former Penn State men's basketball players make NBA Summer League rosters
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Two former Nittany Lions will be featured in the NBA Summer League this month.

Wing Seth Lundy made the summer league roster for the Toronto Raptors, and guard Ace Baldwin Jr. is a part of the Orlando Magic's summer league team.

Lundy wasn't on any NBA or G League roster this past season, and he was most recently waived from a two-way contract with the Los Angeles Clippers in July 2025. He spent time with the Atlanta Hawks and the Clippers during the 2024-25 regular season but didn't appear in any game due to injury.

Lundy was selected in the second round of the 2023 NBA draft by the Hawks, who he played in nine games with during 2023-24. He finished his four-year Penn State career in 2023 after helping the team make a run to the second round of the NCAA Tournament.

The Raptors' summer league schedule features games against the Boston Celtics on July 10, the Houston Rockets on July 11, the Indiana Pacers on July 13 and the Miami Heat on July 16 plus a to be determined fifth game at a later date.

Baldwin is coming off his first season as a professional player, appearing in nine games with the Magic's G League affiliate, the Osceola Magic. He was the No. 3 overall pick in the 2025 G League draft to the Capital City Go-Go, but his draft rights were dealt to the Magic.

Baldwin participated in last year's NBA Summer League as well, playing in two games with the Celtics. He was a two-time Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year during his Penn State career from 2023-25.

During the summer league, the Magic will face the Charlotte Hornets on July 9, the Miami Heat on July 11, the Portland Trail Blazers on July 12 and the Philadelphia 76ers on July 15.

title: ‘It’s going to be a magical evening’ | Corner Room celebrates 100-year anniversary
author: Justin Elliott | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Corner Room restaurant will be celebrating its 100-year anniversary as a State College staple on Friday with “Corner Fest,” featuring a number of events in collaboration with other Hotel State College businesses and borough organizations.

The restaurant was founded in 1855 as Jack’s Road House, when Penn State was still the Farmers’ High School of Pennsylvania, and renamed in 1926. The Corner Room has been under new ownership since 2019, when Pat Croce & Company bought the parent company, Hotel State College & Co.

Stefania Albegiani, the lead server, has been with the restaurant for nearly a decade.

She worked for her dad growing up before opening up a pizzeria with her sister in the early 2000s. The two went their separate ways years ago, and Albegiani, who described herself as always having been a “restaurant girl,” began working at The Corner Room.

“Heartbroken, I found my way to my forever place. That was my blessing in disguise,” Albegiani said.

Albegiani witnessed the restaurant’s makeover under Pat Croce & Company during COVID. She looks through old photos of the restaurant “constantly,” and tells her coworkers about how much it has changed.

She said the improvements they made to the building set it up for many years of success, and the new leadership has been the best thing for The Corner Room.

While working at the restaurant, Albegiani said she has gotten both “old time” guests who come in to reminisce and newcomers passing through.

“I love to hear their stories. Some got engaged in our booths, while some grew up in the area and have been coming in since (they were) young children,” she said.

She has served Ty Burrell and JJ Abrams, both Penn State alums, as well as former football coach James Franklin and his recruits.

As for the fest, she said The Corner Room “has never done anything like this” in her time there.

The “Family Fun Zone” will run from 3 to 6 p.m., consisting of an Alpha Fire Company Fire Truck, a carnival carousel, face painting, balloons, art and a live DJ. From 6:30 p.m. to 12:30 a.m., Zeno’s Pub is hosting two local bands, Mick Mac and Americana band Pure Cane Sugar.

The “Centennial Ceremony” from 6 to 7 p.m. includes live music, a moment of “reflections and commemorations,” a Happy Birthday song and a cake presented by Comet Properties, a local real estate company.

Albegiani said she is excited for all of it, and that it’s going to be “a magical evening.” She’ll be able to enjoy all of the festivities right as she’s done working.

“Having a carousel blows my mind,” Albegiani said. “Without the community, we wouldn’t be what we are today.”

John Harris, a local resident, has been going to The Corner Room since 1961.

After a “major tragedy,” Harris considered leaving the borough for New York City. But one Corner Room waitress, Martha Greenway, was especially nice to him during this difficult time, and he said this was central in his decision to make State College his home.

Harris said the restaurant remains a “central force” in the community, and it’s always attracting new faces.

“It’s become a kind of cultural institution in some way or another,” Harris said. “The wait staff there has always been supportive in one way or another.”

With Pat Croce & Company, the place has drifted from being university-oriented, Harris added. Though he said students are essential to the town, the place has shifted its focus toward becoming more of a family establishment, drawing in more people than previously would have come.

Harris said he has never seen the restaurant host an event like Friday’s fest before, and hopes the heat doesn’t impact it in any way.

“This will be unique,” Harris said.

Lily Persio has been serving at The Corner Room for two years, but she said its long history in State College before her arrival is what makes it special.

“I've had so many guests that have come in and told me that they had their first date there, or they worked there at some point when they were in college. It's really awesome,” Persio, a 2025 advertising and public relations graduate, said.

She said she met some of her best friends working there and enjoys being able to say a place that has been in the borough for a century was a part of her college experience.

Though management comes and goes, the people are still the core of the restaurant, she said, and she finds there to be mutual respect between the borough and the restaurant.

“The fest will be a great opportunity for the community to come together,” Persio said.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: ‘The honor of a lifetime’ | As America turns 250, Penn State athletes reflect on representing their country
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
When Sophia Gladieux was growing up, she looked up to Simone Biles, Michael Jordan and other American athletes, dreaming of the moment she could wear a United States jersey and represent her home country on the field.

A decade later, she earned a spot on the U.S. women’s national field hockey team and competed in the 2024 Paris Olympics. She helped the team to a ninth-place finish in her Olympic debut before returning to Penn State for her final season.

“It’s always an overwhelming sense of pride and joy to be where I am, just representing your country well,” Gladieux told The Daily Collegian. “Going to a school like Penn State, I think I know a little bit about pride and what that means. I had the same feeling wearing a Penn State jersey.”

Although wearing the Team USA jersey never loses its significance, Gladieux said standing on the field before her first Olympic game and singing the national anthem is the moment she remembers the most.

“I get chills thinking about that,” Gladieux said. “It’s really just a full circle moment, to be able to say that you’re part of Team USA.”

Gladieux is just one of several current and former Penn State athletes and coaches reflecting on what it means to represent the country as it approaches its 250th anniversary on Saturday.

Goalkeeper Jenny Rizzo, one of Gladieux’s current U.S. teammates, was drawn to international play by Charlene Morett-Curtiss, her coach at Penn State. Gladieux was also coached by Morett-Curtiss for three years.

Morett-Curtiss was selected to two Olympic teams in 1980 and 1984, taking home bronze in the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics. She played in more than 100 international matches for the U.S., serving as a role model to the Nittany Lion student-athletes.

“The realization that I could follow in her footsteps came when I was in college and her telling stories about her time on the U.S. team,” Rizzo told the Collegian. “Having a coach that makes it tangible for you and encourages you in your dream really instilled a lot of confidence in myself and this dream that I had.”

Years later, Rizzo found herself living that very dream after qualifying for her first Olympic team.

After helping the U.S. clinch its spot in the 2024 Paris Olympics, she stood in a packed, 18,000-seat stadium in India as a light show filled the arena before a match, where the reality of representing the United States finally set in.

“I remember just standing there and being in absolute awe that this is my life,” Rizzo said.

Representing Team USA also gave athletes the opportunity to inspire those hoping to follow in their footsteps. As Gladieux and Rizzo have traveled the world competing on the U.S. women’s field hockey team, they’ve seen a wide range of access to sports for young individuals.

“The cool thing about this day and age is there’s so much opportunity for women in sports in the U.S.,” Rizzo said. “When you look at the history of sport in America, it reminds me that sport is bigger than just putting a ball in the goal. It’s about breaking barriers and giving opportunities for people who are marginalized.”

Now in her fifth year with the national team, Rizzo said she has embraced the opportunity to use her platform to encourage young athletes.

“It’s what our country is all about,” Rizzo said. “No matter where athletes come from, they can represent their country if they want it enough.”

Similarly, Gladieux realized she now occupies the same role Olympians once held for her: Showing younger generations in the field hockey world that it’s possible to achieve their dreams.

For Tessa Janecke, being able to represent the U.S. on the international stage “means everything.”

“To be a part of that legacy, and especially with such a great country … it’s something to be super proud of, so I’m very grateful,” Janecke told the Collegian.

Before finishing one of the most decorated careers in Penn State women’s hockey history, Janecke took her talents to the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics on Team USA, where she helped bring home the third gold medal in U.S. women’s hockey history. She’s the first Penn State athlete to win gold at the Winter Olympics.

“Putting on the jersey just means you’re about to step into an experience that’s much bigger than yourself,” Janecke said.

While Penn State women’s lacrosse coach Kayla Treanor no longer suits up for Team USA, she remains involved with the program as an assistant coach.

Treanor spent more than 15 years representing the U.S. in some capacity and has watched the sport evolve. Along the way, she became one of the best lacrosse players in U.S. history, currently holding five national records.

Now, she hopes to help send the next wave of Penn State athletes to the national team.

“I’m looking forward to the future and getting our athletes on that roster,” Treanor told the Collegian. “It’s quite the experience, and I do hope that our future Nittany Lions are going to be able to experience that as well.”

While today’s athletes spoke of fulfilling childhood dreams, Treanor now hopes to help future Penn Staters realize their own.

“It’s just about pride in representing your country and I think it’s given me an even stronger tie to the United States,” Treanor said. “It makes me really proud to be a citizen.”

title: How the FBS Oversight Committee’s proposed changes for college football would affect Penn State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s been a busy week for Penn State — making the switch from Nike to Adidas, hosting Lift for Life on Wednesday and continuing to move through summer workouts.

But it was also busy for the FBS Oversight Committee, who made several new proposals to impact future FBS seasons.

The biggest of the proposed changes is the calendar for the offseason, as the monetizing college football landscape tries to become more similar to professional sports. However, it also added additional proposals into recruiting and instant replay review.

The proposals were introduced last week and will either be denied or approved in a final committee vote in August. If they pass, they will take effect in 2027.

Here’s a look into each of the rules and their impact on both Penn State and the college football world entirely.

Transfer portal window

This was the first offseason with just one transfer portal window after the NCAA eliminated the spring window, but with this proposal, even more changes to one of the most important parts of the offseason would occur.

The new schedule ultimately modifies each period of the offseason, including the transfer portal window, the spring practice period and preseason training.

Under the proposed legislation, the transfer portal period would move from 15 days to 10 days. The window will open the next business day after Jan. 1, which is Jan. 4 in 2027.

Despite the shortened window, exceptions would still be made for teams who are still competing during or after the period. These players would have five days after the end of their season to enter, and players on teams with coaching changes would get 15 days to enter.

Over the last few years, Penn State had several key players depart via the transfer portal. In 2024, backup quarterback Beau Pribula entered the portal before Penn State’s College Football Playoffs semifinal run, and wide receiver Harrison Wallace III entered a month later.

If the new rules were in place, none of those transfers would’ve happened during the official window.

New spring schedule

With the evolution of NIL pushing college football draws even closer to professional sports, the offseason schedule is expected to take on a similar format.

Teams currently conduct 15 spring practices, culminating in a spring game for many teams, including Penn State.

Instead, there would be two periods of spring practice for a total of 21 on-field practices. The two periods have to be completed within seven weeks and neither window can be over five weeks long.

In addition to the mandatory practice window, teams would designate nine weeks for voluntary practice.

Between the seven weeks of two practice periods and additional nine weeks of voluntary practice, Penn State and other teams will have the opportunity to space out its practice while still utilizing the offseason.

This year, the additional periods would’ve given the Nittany Lions more practice with its large transfer class and spaced out the practices for injury prevention and recovery. While Tony Rojas, Rocco Becht, Brett Eskildsen and others were rehabbing during a majority of the spring, a second practice window could’ve given them an opportunity to practice later on.

Preseason changes

As a result of an increased spring practice schedule, the preseason is expected to be reduced.

Under the proposed guidelines, the preseason would feature 21 practices during 27 days. This is four days less practice than there is now in four less days.

Since teams still have more practices under the new proposals than previously, this further spaces out the offseason and preseason practices. In turn, teams would have more rest immediately before the season.

Other updates

While no official proposal was made, there were discussions on standardizing off-campus recruiting, providing a yearly allotment of days in which the coaching staff could go off-campus to recruit. It would permit up to 16 staff members and the head coach to participate in off-campus recruiting.

The FBS committee is expected to introduce the proposal during its July or August meeting, which would then go into effect in Jan. 2027 if passed. The only change that would take place for the 2026 season is an experimental rule where instant replay would be made available in the coach’s booth in real time.

Each conference will have the opportunity to implement it during the entirety of the conference season, while usage during nonconference games will be decided by the visiting team.

The experimental rule will play a big part in a coach’s decision to challenge a play, as coaches will now have the same view in the booth as a referee before issuing the challenge.

title: What to know before you visit Arts Fest 2026
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Every July, over 125,000 alumni and visitors flock to State College to experience The Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts. The festival, nicknamed Arts Fest, will run from July 8 to July 12 this year.

Arts Fest offers a variety of creative exhibits, performances and activities and is one of the largest non-football alumni weekends for Penn State. This year marks Arts Fest’s 60th anniversary.

Here’s everything you need to know before you visit Arts Fest 2026.

Events

Arts Fest will begin on Wednesday with a Children & Youth Day Sidewalk Sale on South Allen Street. Young artists from the ages of eight through 18 are encouraged to create “individually crafted, original objects,” the Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts website read.

Afterward, there will be the Children & Youth Day Grand Procession, which begins on Old Main Lawn and proceeds to Sidney Friedman Park.

Other events on Wednesday include a yoga lab, caricatures and puppet shows, family friendly improv and a community sing-along.

On Thursday, the 2026 Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition will kick off. Over 300 independent artists and entrepreneurs will present their wares on the street across 20 categories and a wide range of mediums, including jewelry, ceramics and housewares.

Each exhibitor is visited by the festival’s jurors as part of the jury process, and $20,000 in prize money will be awarded amongst the prize-winning artists. On Saturday, the awards ceremony will take place on the Allen Street Stage.

This year, Arts Fest is debuting a brand-new Live Mural Exhibition to celebrate the country’s 250th anniversary. The juried exhibition, “Dreaming American Futures,” will focus on the overarching theme from “The Hill We Climb” by Amanda Gorman, as well as an excerpt from the book

Five artists have been selected to participate in the inaugural event: Jennifer Lonsky and Ruben Rosado of Pink Sky Designs, Scott Kowalski, Elody Gyekis, Kate Murphy, and Margalo Guo.

A variety of events, concerts and activities are available; the festival’s website includes a daily program with more information.

Festival poster

For over half a century, the poster for Arts Fest has either been designed or influenced by Lanny Sommese, the former head of the Penn State Department of Graphic Design, who passed away in 2022.

From 2023 to 2025, his daughter Saige Sommese carried the torch of tradition, “collaborating” with her father by using his concepts to create finished pieces.

This year, Arts Fests asked Saige to create a poster for the festival that was originally hers. The 36” x 24” depicts a graphic birthday cake with candles representing samples from former festival posters.

The posters are freely available at the following locations until the end of the festival, as listed on the website:

Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts on 403 S. Allen St., Suite 205A

Downtown State College Improvement District on 127 S. Fraser St.

State College Framing Company and Gallery on 160 Rolling Ridge Dr.

The Happy Valley Adventure Bureau on 204 W. Beaver Ave.

Hintz Family Alumni Center on Penn State’s campus

After the festival, the poster will exclusively be available on Art Fest’s online store.

Travel

Arts Fest encourages visitors to use Penn State’s West Parking Deck on White Course Drive, which is located near the IST building.

“Parking is $2 for the first hour, $3 for under two hours and $10 for between two and ten hours, cash and credit cards accepted,” according to Art Fest’s travel guide.

There, visitors can use the free bus service to shuttle to and from the festival. The shuttle goes straight from the West Parking Deck to the festival’s center at the corner of College Avenue and Allen Street.

The bus will make an additional stop at the Palmer Museum of Art on Thursday, Friday and Saturday from 9 a.m. to 11 p.m. and Sunday from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m.

Downtown municipal parking garages are available on Beaver Avenue, Fraser Street and Pugh Street. Hourly rates are $2.25 per hour or “any portion thereof,” and the maximum daily rate is $32.

Art Fest has also made a festival map, which is available on their website to print or download.

Accommodations

This year, Arts Fest has chosen Hyatt Place State College, Scholar Hotel State College and The Penn Stater as their preferred hotel partners. All three are located near campus.

The Penn Stater is offering special rates for Arts Fest guests: $179 per night, with a minimum stay of two nights. Attendees must use the code FEST26 for the specialty rates.

Arts Fest also recommends Wyndham Garden State College, Sleep Inn State College near Penn State, Ramada Hotel and Conference Center, Country Inn & Suites, Holiday Inn Express, Days Inn by Wyndham Penn State and Super 8 by Wyndham State College.

A full list of lodging options can also be found on the Happy Valley Adventure Bureau website.

title: 'Keep them on the field' | Resiliency key for Penn State strength coach Reid Kagy
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Reid Kagy was with Iowa State for a total of six years, and he most recently acted as the Cyclones’ director of football strength and conditioning, a role that began in 2023.

That changed when he followed Matt Campbell from Iowa State. Kagy was hired to be the Nittany Lions’ head strength and conditioning coach, replacing Chuck Losey, who followed James Franklin to Virginia Tech.

With his arrival in Happy Valley, Kagy brought a coaching philosophy built on harnessing the capabilities of each individual position group. He wants what he teaches in the weight room to translate directly to the field, and he plans to continue training certain aspects of each group differently depending on specific needs.

“I want to impact play on the field,” Kagy told reporters at Penn State’s annual Lift for Life event. “Power, speed, strength, resiliency, toughness, grit, fortitude — all those things go into that. I want to make sure our guys know what we're doing, why we're doing it, and how it's gonna impact them on the football field.”

Under Losey, Penn State was consistently churning out athletic monsters, and that was a big reason why the Nittany Lions have seen so much success at the NFL draft in recent years.

So it’s up to Kagy to continue that legacy. And his philosophy is doing so reflects plenty of the other coaches that followed Campbell from the Cyclones.

“We're trying to get faster, we're trying to get stronger, we're trying to be more powerful, we're trying to have the ability to repeat efforts,” Kagy said. “We're trying to be disciplined, tough, physical on the field — most violent team in the nation is what we would love to be.”

Team bonding was another point of emphasis for Kagy. He understood coming into this position that making connections between two worlds — Iowa State and Penn State — would be imperative to the team’s success on the field.

Kagy noted that the purpose of winter and summer camps is for building said relationships between the players but also between the players and their coaches. Kagy has been learning each player’s personality to know how to coach them in the most efficient way, citing how he can “push the right buttons at the right time.”

Kagy’s philosophy mirrors that of Campbell’s, as the team’s bond is something that Penn State’s new head coach stressed over the course of the offseason. Campbell reasons that the bond forged between teammates is a crucial aspect of his players’ ability to “fight through adversity.”

“We want to create relationships,” Kagy said. “We want to be a part of that, bridging the gap and making sure this team is together. The most together team is usually the most successful team. We're building that right now.”

The strength and conditioning staff spends eight hours a week with the players, and since time is limited, Kagy is working to ensure that every minute spent training is meaningful.

“If there's something in the program that's either not dialed in towards (impacting play or building relationships), it's probably a waste of our time,” Kagy said. “We don't have endless time with our guys, so we got to focus on the big rocks. We're going to make an impact on the field and keep them on the field.”

title: 2028 QB James Armstrong commits to Penn State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has found its quarterback of the future from Pennsylvania.

The Nittany Lions earned a commitment from James Armstrong on Thursday.

Armstrong, a 4-star, is the No. 10 quarterback in his class and No. 4 player from Pennsylvania, per 247Sports composite. He is Penn State's third commitment in the 2028 class with cornerback Deonte Flemings Jr. and kicker Carter Petri.

Armstrong visited Happy Valley for camp on June 10, participating in drills with quarterbacks coach Jake Waters and other campers.

The Aliquippa native wrapped up his sophomore season with 21 touchdowns against three interceptions. Armstrong threw for 2,232 yards and rushed for another 799 yards and 16 touchdowns.

Armstrong joins 2027 commit Will Wood as future quarterbacks for the Nittany Lions.

title: Potential Penn State offensive breakouts in 2026
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
From a new quarterback to a new playcaller, Penn State’s offense will look very different this season.

While there are several players who are expected to lead the charge in this new era for Penn State, there’s always some unexpected players who end up making a big difference.

Here’s a look at some potential offensive breakout candidates under Year 1 with Matt Campbell.

Koby Howard

One of the most expected breakouts in 2026 is wide receiver Koby Howard, who made a handful of starts in his true freshman season last year. Now in Year 2, Howard is likely to be one of the three starting receivers alongside Iowa State transfers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen.

The Florida native recorded just seven catches during his first year, but averaged 19 yards per catch with his longest reception going for 31 yards.

With veteran receivers in Sowell and Eskildsen in the mix — both who played with quarterback Rocco Becht at Iowa State — Howard will likely be favored less than the two familiar faces. However, he caught the eye of Campbell early and is expected to take off in his sophomore season.

“Koby is a young, talented football player,” Campbell said in April. “He’s got great potential and we’re really grateful to have him back.”

Quinton Martin Jr.

Penn State’s running back room will undoubtedly look much different without stars Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen. The exact depth chart is to be determined with Martin and transfers James Peoples and Carson Hansen, but the returning Nittany Lion is expected to make a much bigger contribution this season.

Last season, Martin’s sole contribution came in the Pinstripe Bowl against Clemson, where he recorded 103 rushing yards on 20 attempts, including a career-best 21-yard rush.

During the offseason, the Pennsylvania native has worked with new running backs coach Savon Huggins to improve his fitness and skillset, as well as adding some weight to his now 212-pound frame.

“I think I’ve taken a big step forward in (being a) running back all-around, just getting more downhill, pad-level, producing and just staying consistent,” Martin said in April.

Malachi Goodman

Former 4-star recruit and No. 9 offensive tackle in his class, Malachi Goodman did not see the field in his first year at Penn State. Now, he’s expected to be the starting left tackle as a redshirt freshman.

Both Goodman and his position group as a whole have drawn a lot of praise from both Ryan Clanton and Campbell, who’ve called the offensive tackles one of the best positions on the field.

“Boy, I don’t know if I could be any more impressed with what I’ve seen from that group,” Campbell said in April. “Goodman’s 330 (pounds) and he looks like he’s 250 (pounds). He’s smooth. He may be a redshirt freshman, but I feel like his maturity of physical ability is way beyond that.”

Cooper Cousins

Entering Penn State, there was high praise and potential for Cooper Cousins, who was selected as a true freshman preseason All-American by 247Sports. While he never got to establish himself, making just two starts through two seasons, he showed flashes of success.

Cousins will without a doubt take on a starting role on Penn State’s offensive line this season, likely at right guard, and will be able to cement those successful moments as a starter.

Despite the new faces along the offensive line, Cousins has emerged as a leader. The junior took the opportunity to play a variety of positions at Blue-White Practice, showcasing his abilities on the field.

“(Clanton) asked me and really all the offensive line, what he wanted to do and get better at this spring,” Cousins said after Blue-White Practice. “Me personally, I wanted to play all the positions so I can learn all of them. That way I know what my tackles are thinking, what my centers are thinking, and all that.”

Andrew Rappleyea

After playing in just three games with no stats in his first two seasons — including a season-ending injury his redshirt freshman season — tight end Andrew Rappleyea made a big jump in 2025.

Rappleyea earned eight starts and appeared in another five games, recording 20 receptions for 180 yards and three touchdowns during his redshirt sophomore season.

Despite the increased playing time, Rappleyea was still considered the No. 3 tight end on the team behind Khalil Dinkins and Luke Reynolds. This year, he’s expected to take the second spot behind Iowa State transfer Benjamin Brahmer.

title: How the One Big Beautiful Bill Act changes student loans
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Student loan provisions from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) began on Wednesday, following a final rule by the U.S. Department of Education on May 1 to implement most of the law.

The OBBBA was signed into law on July 4, 2025 by President Donald Trump. Undergraduate, graduate and professional students can all expect changes to loan limits and repayment plans.

Here’s everything you need to know about student loans under the OBBBA.

Lifetime Limits

All new undergraduate, graduate and professional student borrowers have a lifetime maximum loan limit of $257,500. This includes all subsidized and unsubsidized loans received for undergraduate studies, graduate and professional studies, as well as PLUS loans received prior to entering graduate or professional studies.

“Even if you pay off or receive a discharge on any amount of your loans, you can’t borrow more than the lifetime maximum loan limit,” a statement on the Federal Student Aid’s website read.

However, full-time students and part-time students will have different annual loan limits. Part-time students’ annual loan limits will be adjusted based on enrollment level.

New Undergraduate Borrowers

There are a few guidelines under OBBBA for new undergraduate students expecting loan disbursements starting on or after Wednesday.

Loan Limits

While annual and aggregate loan limits will not change, academic institutions may set a loan limit lower than the legal maximum. OBBBA allows this as long as all borrowers in the same program of study are given the same limit.

Direct PLUS Loans

Parents who receive Direct PLUS Loans will now have reduced borrowing eligibility. Dependent undergraduates can receive up to $20,000 per year from their parents, and the overall aggregate limit of $65,000 includes “any amount paid in full or discharged,” according to the Federal Student Aid statement.

Parents reaching the annual or aggregate limit for PLUS loans prevents them from receiving additional PLUS Loans and doesn’t increase their dependent’s unsubsidized loan limit.

If one or more parents have adverse credit and cannot receive a PLUS loan, their dependent’s annual Direct Unsubsidized Loan limit will increase.

New Graduate Student Borrowers

Loan Limits

Academic institutions may set a loan limit lower than the legal maximum for graduate students, but all borrowers in the same program of study must be given the same limit.

While loan limits for graduate and professional students were previously identical, graduate students will now have different limits from students enrolled in programs of study leading to professional degrees.

Graduate students will have a reduced aggregate loan limit compared to those active before Wednesday. Those enrolled in graduate programs may receive unsubsidized loans up to $20,500 per year and up to $100,000 in total if they have never been professional students.

“This aggregate loan limit won’t include loans received for undergraduate study, but includes all Direct Subsidized and Unsubsidized Loans made for graduate study,” according to the Federal Office of Student Aid.

For students who were professional students but are now graduate students, unsubsidized loans of up to $200,000 (minus disbursements for their professional program) are available. This figure includes all subsidized and unsubsidized loans for graduate or professional study.

Direct PLUS Loans are no longer available for graduate students.

New Professional Student Borrowers

Professional degrees are typically doctoral level degrees requiring “at least six academic years of postsecondary education to complete, including at least two years of post-baccalaureate coursework, as well as generally requiring professional licensure to begin practice in the intended field,” according to the Federal Student Aid website.

Professional degrees, as defined by the Federal Aid Office, fall into the following programs: Pharmacy (Pharm.D.); Dentistry (D.D.S. or D.M.D.); Veterinary Medicine (D.V.M.); Chiropractic (DC or DCM); Law (L.L.B. or J.D.); Medicine (M.D.); Optometry (O.D.); Osteopathic Medicine (D.O.); Podiatry (D.P.M, D.P. or Pod.D.); Theology (M.Div. or M.H.L.); and Clinical Psychology (Psy.D. or Ph.D.).

Loan Limits

Professional degree seekers will receive loan limits that differ from those given to graduate students. They will be eligible for up to $50,000 in unsubsidized annual loans.

Annual and aggregate loan limits for professional students have increased in comparison to pre-July figures, according to the Federal Student Aid statement.

If professional students were not previously enrolled in graduate programs, they may receive unsubsidized loans up to $200,000. If they were previously enrolled in graduate programs, they may receive unsubsidized loans up to $200,000 minus any disbursement received for graduate school.

Direct PLUS Loans are no longer available for professional students.

Repayment Plans for Undergraduate, Graduate and Professional Students

Students whose loans are disbursed from July 1 onwards must repay all eligible direct loans under the Repayment Assistance Plan (RAP) or the Tiered Standard Plan.

Students with direct subsidized loans, direct unsubsidized loans, direct PLUS loans for graduate or professional students or direct consolidation loans that do not include parent PLUS loans are allowed to repay their loans with either RAP or the Tiered Standard Plan.

Parent PLUS loans or direct consolidation loans including parent PLUS loans may only be repaid under the Tiered Standard Plan.
